elements.sent-2411-001: The recent work of Brown et al. (arXiv:2411.03447) demonstrated that the low-temperature evaporation rate of a large near-extremal charged black hole is significantly reduced from semiclassical expectations.
elements.sent-2411-002: The quantum corrections responsible for the deviation come from Schwarzian modes of an emergent Jackiw-Teitelboim gravity description of the near-horizon geometry of the black hole.
elements.sent-2411-003: Using a one-parameter family of non-perturbative Airy completions, we extend these results to incorporate non-perturbative effects.
elements.sent-2411-004: At large parameter value, the non-perturbative evaporation rate is even smaller than the perturbative JT gravity results.
elements.sent-2411-005: The disparity becomes especially pronounced at very low energies, where the non-perturbative neutral Hawking flux is suppressed by a double exponential in the entropy of the black hole, effectively stopping its evaporation until the next charged particle is emitted via the Schwinger effect.
elements.sent-2411-006: We also explore an alternative family of Bessel completions for which the non-perturbative energy flux exceeds the perturbative JT gravity prediction.
elements.sent-2411-007: Jackiw-Teitelboim (JT) gravity \cite{Jackiw:1984je,Teitelboim:1983ux and the associated Schwarzian dynamics serve as a robust framework for systematically incorporating quantum gravity corrections into perturbative QFT computations on a black hole background \cite{Penington:2019kki,Saad:2019pqd .}}
elements.sent-2411-008: An interesting recent development is the computation of the low-temperature evaporation rate of a large near-extremal black hole \cite{Brown:2024ajk , where it was shown that the semiclassical picture of Hawking radiation breaks down under the following conditions (in Planck units), \bea E_i \equiv M-Q\lesssim E_{\brk \equiv \frac{1 {Q^3}\, .}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: When energies are below $E_{\brk}$, these modes are strongly coupled, and the density of states gets modified.
elements.sent-2411-010: In particular, the leading perturbative density of states $\rho_0(E)$ goes to zero when $E$ goes to zero, without producing a mass gap in the spectrum \cite{Iliesiu:2020qvm .}
elements.sent-2411-011: The density of states dropping to zero at $E=0$ has important consequences for the computation of the black hole evaporation rate.
elements.sent-2411-012: When $E_i \ll E_\brk$, the Schwarzian spectrum constrains the emission of particles with energies greater than $E_i$.
elements.sent-2411-013: This sharply contrasts the semiclassical behaviour, where no such restriction exists.
elements.sent-2411-014: The resulting modifications to the evaporation rates were analyzed extensively in \cite{Brown:2024ajk .}
elements.sent-2411-015: In this paper, we study the effect of non-perturbative corrections on these emission rates using non-perturbative completions of JT gravity based random matrix models as pioneered in \cite{Saad:2019lba and further developed in \cite{Johnson:2019eik .}}
elements.sent-2411-016: We start with a one-parameter family of Airy completions that were introduced in \cite{Johnson:2019eik .}
elements.sent-2411-017: The parameter brings a new energy scale, $\mu$, into the problem which affects the rate of evaporation at low energies.
elements.sent-2411-018: These Airy completions suffer from a non-perturbative instability which can, however, be avoided via more intricate matrix model constructions as described in \cite{Johnson:2019eik .}
elements.sent-2411-019: We mainly focus on the Airy case, due to its relative simplicity, and address the non-perturbative instability by simply truncating the Airy density of states at $E_i=0$.
elements.sent-2411-020: Towards the end of the paper we also consider a family of Bessel completions, that are argued to be stable in \cite{Johnson:2019eik .}
elements.sent-2411-021: In figure \ref{airyplot , we have plotted the density of states of a few non-perturbative Airy completions.}
elements.sent-2411-022: The plots exhibit two new features that are absent in the perturbative density of states.
elements.sent-2411-023: The plots contain wiggles that die off at large energies, a direct consequence of the discreteness of the underlying spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-024: The other significant feature is an exponential suppression of the density of states at low energies, controlled by the emergent energy scale $\mu$.
elements.sent-2411-025: The combination of the wiggles and the low energy density of states alters the evaporation rate compared to the perturbative result of \cite{Brown:2024ajk .\footnote{In this paper, we will reserve the term `perturbative' for leading order disc computations in JT gravity.}}
elements.sent-2411-026: We will use the term `semiclassical' to refer to perturbative QFT calculations on the black hole background.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth]{airy.pdf} \caption{The plot of the density of states our non-perturbative Airy completions of JT gravity, with the leading perturbative result shown in red. These completions, labelled by a parameter $\mu$, exhibit an exponential decay at low energies.} \label{airyplot}
elements.sent-2411-027: Consider a black hole with integer spin.
elements.sent-2411-028: The low-temperature evaporation will be dominated by the emission of di-photons \cite{Brown:2024ajk .}
elements.sent-2411-029: When $\mu \gg e^{-2S_0/3 E_\brk$, we find the non-perturbative evaporation rates to be: \bea \frac{\dd M {\dd t \sim \begin{dcases -(M-Q)^5, M-Q \gg E_{\mathrm{brk \,, -(M-Q)^{\frac{19 {2 , \mu \ll M-Q \ll E_{\mathrm{brk \,, -\exp\left(-\frac{4e^{S_0}\mu^{3/2 {E_{\brk ^{3/2 }\right) (M-Q)^{9 , M-Q \ll \mu \,.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-030: When $M-Q \ll \mu$, the evaporation rate is suppressed by a double exponential in the entropy of the black hole.
elements.sent-2411-031: This is because the exponentially decaying tail of the density of states essentially cuts off the emission of all particles (see figure \ref{airyplot ).}
elements.sent-2411-032: Therefore, the black hole has more or less stopped evaporating.
elements.sent-2411-033: The black hole also has a probability of emitting a positron via the Schwinger effect.
elements.sent-2411-034: Upon the emission of a positron, the black hole is pushed away from extremality.
elements.sent-2411-035: Positron emission can, in principle, compete with our non-perturbative corrections, as it may push the black hole out of extremality before it reaches the energy scale necessary to observe the new effects.
elements.sent-2411-036: However, since positron emission is important only at energy scales of the order of $e^{-O\left(\sqrt{S_0}\right)}$ \cite{Brown:2024ajk , our non-perturbative effects at $M-Q \gg \mu$ become noticeable well before the black hole emits a positron and is driven away from extremality.}
elements.sent-2411-037: When $\mu \ll e^{-2S_0/3 E_\brk$, non-perturbative corrections become important at $E_i \sim e^{-2S_0/3 E_\brk$.}}
elements.sent-2411-038: We find that non-perturbative emission in fact exceeds the perturbative result of \cite{Brown:2024ajk at such low energies but, unfortunately, positron emission is also relevant at these energies and is likely to move the black hole away from extremality before any non-perturbative enhancement of the emission rate can be observed.}
elements.sent-2411-039: One might have expected the emergent non-perturbative energy scale $\mu$ to always be smaller than $O(e^{-S_0 )$ in suitable units of energy.}
elements.sent-2411-040: However, we note that the Airy completions presented here should be viewed as simple avatars of more realistic models of non-perturbative JT gravity \cite{Johnson:2019eik .}
elements.sent-2411-041: Therefore, one should ask whether non-perturbative completions of JT gravity exist in which the analog of the parameter $\mu$ is not exponentially suppressed.
elements.sent-2411-042: We find the answer to be affirmative, as there are solutions to the master string equation where a specific half-integer parameter, $\Gamma$, is turned on (see figure 9 of \cite{Johnson:2020heh and the discussion around it).}
elements.sent-2411-043: The non-perturbative sector of these models does not contain D-branes, and as a result, non-perturbative corrections are not exponentially suppressed in the inverse of the string coupling constant \cite{Johnson:2006ux .}
elements.sent-2411-044: This justifies considering larger values of $\mu$.
elements.sent-2411-045: We can also perform a Bessel completion of the density of states.
elements.sent-2411-046: In this case, the eigenvalues of the corresponding random matrix `pile' up at $E=0$, and this is expected to capture the low energy physics of stable non-perturbative completions of JT gravity and its supergravity counterparts \cite{Stanford:2019vob,Johnson:2019eik,Johnson:2020heh .}
elements.sent-2411-047: In Section~\ref{sec:bessel below, we find that the pile-up of eigenvalues leads to an energy flux at low energies that significantly exceeds the perturbative result.}
elements.sent-2411-048: Let us end this introduction with the following remark.
elements.sent-2411-049: As proposed in \cite{Brown:2024ajk , the energy flux from a large near-extremal charged black hole can in principle be measured using a simple spectrograph if a large near-extremal astrophysical black hole is ever observed.}
elements.sent-2411-050: The main result of the present paper is that such observations would not only demonstrate a novel breakdown of semi-classical gravity, as was emphasised in \cite{Brown:2024ajk , but offer a direct window onto non-perturbative quantum effects.}
elements.sent-2411-051: They could even allow observers to distinguish between different non-perturbative completions of JT gravity.
elements.sent-2411-052: Given that the energy scale $\mu$ is sufficiently large in some of these completions, the distinction could be made without having to wait for exponentially long time scales.
elements.sent-2411-053: The spherical reduction of Einstein-Maxwell theory in the near-horizon region of a near-extremal Reissner-Nordstr\"{o m black hole results in JT gravity \cite{Navarro-Salas:1999zer,Nayak:2018qej , whose action is given by \bea I = S_0 +\frac{1 {2 \int d^2 x \sqrt{g \Phi\left(R+\frac{2 {\ell^2}\right)+\Phi_{b \int_{\partial_M d u \sqrt{h}\left(K-\frac{1 {\ell}\right)\, .}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-054: The extremal entropy and the AdS$_2$ length scale are related to the extremal charge $Q$ through the relations \bea S_0 = \pi Q^2\,, \qquad \ell =Q \,.
elements.sent-2411-055: The Schwarzian description offers analytic control and the theory can be non-perturbatively completed using the connection between JT gravity and matrix models.
elements.sent-2411-056: The two-dimensional dilaton theory is dual to a double-scaled Hermitian matrix model \cite{Saad:2019lba , whose leading perturbative density of states is given by \bea \rho_0(E, Q)=\frac{e^{S_0(Q) {2 \pi^2 E_{\text {brk \sinh \left(2 \pi \sqrt{2 \sqrt{\frac{E {E_{\text {brk }\right) \Theta(E)\, .}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-057: We extend this analysis by including non-perturbative corrections.
elements.sent-2411-058: There is no unique non-perturbative completion of JT gravity (see \cite{Saad:2019lba,Johnson:2019eik,Collier:2023cyw for some examples).}
elements.sent-2411-059: We will use an Airy completion, that is central to the work of \cite{Saad:2019lba , to model the non-perturbative completions in \cite{Johnson:2019eik .}}
elements.sent-2411-060: The exact Airy density of states is given by \bea \rho_\text{Ai (E) = h^{-\frac{2 {3 }\left[\Ai^{\prime (\zeta)^2-\zeta\Ai(\zeta)^2\right] \Theta(E)\, , \eea where $\zeta = -h^{-\frac{2 {3 \left(E-\mu\right)$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-061: The $\Theta$ function in \eqref{exactairydos simulates the natural truncation at $E=0$ of the density of states in \cite{Johnson:2019eik , as was explained in section~\ref{introduction .}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: The parameter $\mu$ is chosen to be positive to model key features of the non-perturbative completions discussed in \cite{Johnson:2019eik .}
elements.sent-2411-063: When $E \gg \mu$, the function asymptotes to (see figure \ref{airyplot ) \bea \rho_{\Ai (E)\simeq \frac{\sqrt{E {\pi h}\, .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-064: To ensure that the non-perturbative completion eventually asymptotes to \eqref{perturbativeairydos , we will assume that \bea \mu \ll E_\brk\, .. \eea.}
elements.figure-2411-002: \begin{subfigure}{0.49\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{massless_comparison.pdf} \caption{} \label{masslesscompplot} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.49\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{massless_final.pdf} \caption{} \label{masslessfluxplots} \end{subfigure} \caption{Plots of energy flux from massless scalar particle emission when $E_i \sim \mu$. (a) The non-perturbative density of states has an exponentially decaying tail that stops the emission of particles of energy in the vicinity of $E_i$. (b) The total non-perturbative energy flux is smaller than that of the perturbative and semiclassical results.} \label{masslessplots}

elements.section-2411-005: When Eigenvalues Pile Up: The Bessel Completion
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=1.02\linewidth]{bessel.pdf} \caption{(a). The plot of the density of states of a Bessel completion. The eigenvalues pile up against a ``wall'', and the value of $\rho(E)$ at $E=0$ depends on the parameter $\mu$. (b). The non-perturbative flux lies between the semiclassical and perturbative plots.} \label{besselplot}
elements.sent-2411-121: Another curious non-perturbative completion of JT gravity is where the eigenvalues pile up at $E=0$ (see figure \ref{besselplot ).}
elements.sent-2411-122: Non-perturbative completions of this sort were found in \cite{Johnson:2019eik and have the favourable behaviour that they do not have any incursions into the $E<0$ forbidden region.}
elements.sent-2411-123: The incursion is stopped by an infinite ``wall,'' which emerges from integrating out angular variables in complex matrix models \cite{Morris:1990bw,Dalley:1991qg .}
elements.sent-2411-124: We model this behaviour by working with the following density of states: \bea \rho(E) = \rho_0(E)+ \frac{E_\brk {2\pi h \sqrt{E+\mu }\, , \eea where $h$ is given by \eqref{hdef and $\rho_0(E)$ is the perturbative JT gravity density of states.}}}
elements.sent-2411-125: The second term in the above expression asymptotes to the low-energy density of state in the Bessel models of \cite{Johnson:2019eik , which in turn are expected to be a good description of the low energy behaviour of JT supergravity \cite{Stanford:2019vob,Johnson:2020mwi .}}
elements.sent-2411-126: The parameter $\mu$ controls the value of the density of states at $E=0$.
elements.sent-2411-127: The piling up of eigenvalues results in an increased flux at low energies.
elements.sent-2411-128: To see this, let us revisit the massless scalar particle emission computation.
elements.sent-2411-129: When $E_i \ll \mu$, we can integrate the flux to obtain \bea \frac{\dd E {\dd t \simeq \frac{E_\brk^{3/2 r_+^2 {3 \sqrt{2 \pi ^2 \sqrt{\mu E_i^3\,.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-130: In \cite{Brown:2024ajk it was shown that at late times the flux of neutral radiation from a large charged black hole will be significantly reduced compared to semiclassical expectations.}
elements.sent-2411-131: Overall, our results indicate that any measurement sensitive enough to pick up the perturbative JT gravity signal predicted by \cite{Brown:2024ajk could in principle also differentiate among various suggested non-perturbative completions and place constraints on their parameter values.}
